title: EXPOSÉ IX GROUPS OF MULTIPLICATIVE TYPE: HOMOMORPHISMS INTO A GROUP SCHEME

pages.title: EXPOSÉ IX GROUPS OF MULTIPLICATIVE TYPE: HOMOMORPHISMS INTO A GROUP SCHEME

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ IX
GROUPS OF MULTIPLICATIVE TYPE: HOMOMORPHISMS INTO A GROUP SCHEMEby A. Grothendieck
1. Definitions

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme. We say that G is a group of multiplicative type if G is locally diagonalizable in the sense of the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology (cf. IV 6.3), i.e. if for every \(s \in S\), there exist an open neighborhood U of \(s\) and a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(S' \to U\) such that \(G' = G \times_S S'\) is a diagonalizable S′-group (I 4.4 and VIII 1.1).

— We say that G is of quasi-isotrivial multiplicative type if it is even locally diagonalizable in the sense of the étale topology (IV 6.3), i.e. if in the preceding definition, we may even take \(S' \to U\) étale surjective, or equivalently (which comes to the same thing, as one sees by taking the sum scheme of the S′ attached to the various \(s \in S\)) if there exists \(S' \to S\) étale and surjective such that \(G' = G \times_S S'\) is a locally diagonalizable S′-group.

— If we may even choose \(S' \to S\) étale surjective and finite, we say that G is of isotrivial multiplicative type.

— Finally, we say that G is a group of locally trivial (resp. locally isotrivial) multiplicative type if every \(s \in S\) admits an open neighborhood U such that \(G \times_S U\) is a diagonalizable U-group (resp. a group of isotrivial multiplicative type, i.e. there exists a finite étale surjective morphism \(S' \to U\) such that \(G \times_S S'\) is a diagonalizable S′-group).

blocks.title: Comments
blocks.html:
Comments 1.2

Note that the five preceding notions are all deduced from the notion of diagonalizable group by the process of localization, in the sense of five different "topologies" associated with (Sch). We agree in general that when the word "locally" is not made explicit by specifying the topology under consideration, the Zariski topology is meant, which conforms to the accepted terminology. In the terminology introduced here, "of locally trivial multiplicative type" is equivalent to "locally diagonalizable" of VIII 1.1; likewise "trivial" translates as "diagonalizable". Among the five notions introduced, we have the following diagram of implications, which results from the corresponding relations among the topologies involved:

\[ \xymatrix{ & \text{general multiplicative type} & \\ & \text{quasi-isotrivial} \ar@{=>}[u] & \\ & \text{locally isotrivial} \ar@{=>}[u] & \\ \text{isotrivial} \ar@{=>}[ur] & & \text{locally trivial} \ar@{=>}[ul] \\ & \text{trivial} \ar@{=>}[ul] \ar@{=>}[ur] & } \]

From the practical point of view, let us point out at once that all the groups of multiplicative type that we shall encounter will be quasi-isotrivial; thus, we shall see in the next exposé (X 4.5) that when G is of finite type over S, then G is automatically quasi-isotrivial, but we shall give examples where it is not locally isotrivial. We shall likewise see there that G may be locally trivial without being isotrivial, nor a fortiori trivial (which easily implies that the inclusions in the preceding diagram are strict).

On the other hand, we shall also see there (X 5.16) that when S is locally noetherian and normal (more generally geometrically unibranch), then every group of multiplicative type of finite type over S is necessarily isotrivial, and moreover trivial as soon as it is locally trivial (or merely trivial on a dense open set). This explains why most of the groups of multiplicative type encountered in practice will no doubt be isotrivial, all the more so as we shall see later that the maximal tori of semisimple group schemes are automatically isotrivial.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3

Let S, G be as in 1.1. We say that G is a torus if it is locally isomorphic, in the sense of the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, to a group of the form \(\mathbb{G}_m^r\) (where r is an integer \(\geqslant 0\)).

With the notation of 1.1, this therefore means that we may choose S′ so that G′ is isomorphic to a group of the form \((\mathbb{G}_{m,S'})^r\). Note that the integer r depends on \(s \in S\); it is the dimension of the fiber \(G_s = G \otimes_S \kappa(s)\). It is a locally constant function of s, as one verifies at once. It is worthwhile to generalize this remark:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4

Let G be a diagonalizable group scheme over a field k, so G is isomorphic to a group \(\mathbf{D}_k(M)\), where M is an ordinary commutative group, defined up to isomorphism by this condition, more precisely \(M \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-gr.}}(G, \mathbb{G}_{m,k})\) (VIII 1.3). The isomorphism class of M is called the type of the diagonalizable group G; it is evidently invariant under extension of the base field.

If now G is a group scheme of multiplicative type over an arbitrary prescheme S, then for every \(s \in S\), there exists an extension k of \(\kappa(s)\) such that \(G \times_S \mathrm{Spec}(k)\) is a diagonalizable k-group1; its type is then independent of the extension chosen, by the preceding remark, and will be called the type of G at s, or type of \(G_s\). In particular, if G itself is diagonalizable, hence isomorphic to a group of the form \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\), then for every \(s \in S\), the type of G at s equals the class of the group M.

In general, if G is a group of multiplicative type over S and M an ordinary commutative group, we shall say that G is of type M if it is of type M at every point \(s \in S\), in other words if G is locally isomorphic to \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\) in the sense of the faithfully flat and quasi-compact topology.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.4.1

2 a) One sees immediately that for a given group of multiplicative type G over S, the function \(s \mapsto\) type of \(G_s\) is locally constant on S: indeed, with the notation of 1.1, if G′ is of type M, then G is of type M over the neighborhood U of s. It follows that we have a canonical partition of S into a sum of preschemes \(S_i\), such that for every i, \(G_{S_i}\) is of type \(M_i\), where the \(M_i\) are pairwise non-isomorphic commutative groups.

b) In particular, if S is connected, then the type of the fibers of G is constant, i.e. there exists a commutative group M such that G is of type M. Finally, if G is a torus, the type of G at s is characterized by the integer \(\dim G_s\) (indeed, \(G_s\) is of type \(\mathbb{Z}^n\), where \(n = \dim G_s\)).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.5

a) It is trivial from Definitions 1.1 and 1.3 that they are "compatible with base extension". Thus, if G is a group prescheme over S, and \(S' \to S\) a base-change morphism, then if G is of multiplicative type (resp. of isotrivial multiplicative type, etc.), the same holds for G′.

b) When moreover \(S' \to S\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, then if G′ is of multiplicative type, resp. a torus, the same holds for G. If moreover \(S' \to S\) is étale (resp. finite étale), and G′ quasi-isotrivial (resp. isotrivial), the same holds for G.

c) Finally, returning to an arbitrary base-change morphism \(f : S' \to S\), if \(s' \in S'\) and \(s = f(s')\), then the type of G′ at \(s'\) equals that of G at s, since \(G'_{s'} = G_s \otimes_{\kappa(s)} \kappa(s')\).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: Indeed, by hypothesis there exist an open neighborhood U of s and a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(S' \to U\) such that \(G_{S'}\) is diagonalizable; then for every \(s' \in S'\) above s, \(\kappa(s')\) is an extension of \(\kappa(s)\) and \(G_{s'} = G \times_S \mathrm{Spec}(\kappa(s'))\) is diagonalizable.
N.D.E.: We have given the number 1.4.1 to the remarks that follow, for later references.

pages.title: 2. Extension of certain properties of diagonalizable groups to groups of multiplicative type

blocks.html: 2. Extension of certain properties of diagonalizable groups to groups of multiplicative type

The extensions in question are essentially trivial consequences of the results of the preceding exposé, taking into account Definitions 1.1 and the "local" nature (in the sense of the faithfully flat and quasi-compact topology) of the results in question.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.0

3 We denote by \(\mathscr{M}\) the set of faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

Let G be a group of multiplicative type over a prescheme S. We have the following:

  1. a)

    G is faithfully flat over S, and affine over S (a fortiori quasi-compact over S).

  2. b)

    G of finite type over \(S \Leftrightarrow\) G of finite presentation over \(S \Leftrightarrow\) for every \(s \in S\), the type of G at s is given by a finitely generated commutative group.

  3. c)

    G finite over \(S \Leftrightarrow\) for every \(s \in S\), the type of G at s is given by a finite commutative group \(\Leftrightarrow\) (if S is quasi-compact) G is of finite type over S and is killed by an integer \(n > 0\).

  4. c′)

    G integral over \(S \Leftrightarrow\) for every \(s \in S\), the type of G at s is given by a torsion commutative group.

  5. d)

    G is the unit group \(\Leftrightarrow\) for every \(s \in S\), the type of G at s is given by the zero commutative group.

  6. e)

    G is smooth over \(S \Leftrightarrow\) for every \(s \in S\), the type of G at s is given by a finitely generated commutative group whose torsion subgroup has order prime to the characteristic of \(\kappa(s)\).

These statements result from VIII 2.1, taking into account that the properties in question descend by faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms (cf. SGA 1, VIII or EGA IV2, §2).
Using VIII 3.5, we obtain likewise:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2

Let G be a group of multiplicative type and of finite type over S; then for every integer \(n \neq 0\), the kernel \({}_n G\) of \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_G\) is a group of multiplicative type finite over S.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

Let G be a group of multiplicative type over the prescheme S, acting freely on the right on the S-prescheme X affine over S. Then:

  1. a)

    The equivalence relation defined by G in X is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective (IV 3.4); moreover \(Y = X/G\) is affine over S.

  2. b)

    If moreover X is of finite presentation (resp. of finite type) over S, the same holds for Y.

The first assertion results from VIII 5.1, which treats the case where G is diagonalizable, and from IV 3.5.2, which allows us to reduce to it, taking into account that faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms \(S' \to S\) are morphisms of effective descent for the fibered category of schemes affine over others, i.e. for every Y′ affine over S′, equipped with a descent datum relative to \(S' \to S\), this descent datum is effective, i.e. Y′ comes from a Y affine over S, cf. SGA 1, VIII 2.1.

For the second assertion, we are likewise reduced to the diagonalizable case VIII 5.8, since the finiteness conditions under consideration descend by faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms (SGA 1, VIII 3.3 and 3.64).
Proceeding as in VIII, Corollaries 5.5 to 5.7, we draw from 2.3:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Under the conditions of 2.3, the graph morphism \[ X \underset{S}{\times} G \longrightarrow X \underset{S}{\times} X \] is a closed immersion. For every section \(\sigma\) of X over S, the corresponding morphism \(G \to X\), \(g \mapsto \sigma \cdot g\) is a closed immersion.
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a monomorphism of S-group preschemes, with G of multiplicative type and H affine over S. Then u is a closed immersion, \(H/G = Y\) exists and is affine over S, and finally H is a principal homogeneous bundle over Y with group \(G_Y\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.6

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a monomorphism of S-group preschemes, G being of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, H being of finite presentation over S and separated over S. Then one can show that u is a closed immersion, using VIII 7.12 (see Remark VIII 7.13 b)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a homomorphism of S-groups of multiplicative type, with H of finite type over S. Then:

  1. (i)

    \(G' = \mathrm{Ker}\, u\) is an S-group of multiplicative type, the equivalence relation defined by G′ in G is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective, hence (IV 3.4) u factors as \[ G \longrightarrow G/G' = I \longrightarrow H, \] where \(I \to H\) is a monomorphism5 of S-groups; I is of multiplicative type, the equivalence relation in H defined by I is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective, consequently \(H' = H/I\) exists, and moreover H′ is of multiplicative type.

  2. (ii)

    H′ and I are of finite type, and so is G′ if G is.

Proof. Proceeding as for 2.3, we are reduced to the case where G and H are diagonalizable, and then 2.7 reduces to VIII 3.4.
Let us note the following corollaries:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8

a) Let S be a prescheme. Then the category of S-groups of multiplicative type and of finite type is an abelian category.

b) Let \(u : G \to H\) be a homomorphism in this category; for it to be a monomorphism (resp. an epimorphism, resp. an isomorphism) in this category, it is necessary and sufficient that u be a monomorphism of preschemes (resp. that u be faithfully flat, resp. that u be an isomorphism of preschemes).

It suffices to note that the product of two S-groups of multiplicative type is again of multiplicative type (which is immediate), and evidently of finite type over S if the two factors are. The rest of 2.8 results immediately from 2.7; the details are left to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.9

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a homomorphism of S-groups of multiplicative type and of finite type. Let U be the set of points \(s \in S\) such that \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is a monomorphism (resp. faithfully flat, resp. an isomorphism). Then U is both open and closed, and the induced homomorphism \(u_U : G_U \to H_U\) is a monomorphism (resp. faithfully flat, resp. an isomorphism).

Let P (resp. Q) be the kernel (resp. cokernel) of u. Thanks to 2.7, Q exists and P and Q are of multiplicative type; moreover the formation of P and of Q commutes with every base change \(S' \to S\), in particular with the formation of fibers. On the other hand, u is a monomorphism (resp. faithfully flat, resp. an isomorphism) if and only if \(P = 0\) (resp. \(Q = 0\), resp. \(P = Q = 0\)). We are therefore reduced, thanks to these remarks, to verifying the following: if R is an S-group of multiplicative type, then the set U of \(s \in S\) such that \(R_s = 0\) is open and closed, and \(R_U = 0\). Now this is contained in Remark 1.4.1 a).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.10

Let G be an S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type, H and H′ two subgroups of multiplicative type.

a) \(H'' = H \cap H'\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type of G.

b) Let U be the set of \(s \in S\) such that \(H_s \subseteq H'_s\) (resp. \(H_s = H'_s\)). Then U is open and closed, and \(H_U \subseteq H'_U\) (resp. \(H_U = H'_U\)).

Of course, the intersection sign in \(H \cap H'\) denotes the intersection in the functorial sense, i.e. \(H \times_G H'\), which is evidently a sub-S-group of G; likewise the inclusion (resp. equality) sign denotes the order relation (resp. equality) between subfunctors of H (and not inclusion (resp. equality) of the underlying sets).

Applying first 2.7 to the inclusion morphism \(H \to G\), we find that H is of finite type; likewise H′ is of finite type. It then results from 2.8 that \(H \cap H'\) is of multiplicative type and of finite type (note that the canonical functor from the category considered in 2.8 to the category \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\) commutes with finite projective limits). The formation of \(H \cap H'\) commutes with base extension, in particular with the formation of fibers. On the other hand, \(H \subseteq H'\) (resp. \(H = H'\)) is equivalent to \(H'' = H\) (resp. \(H'' = H\) and \(H'' = H'\)). Consider then the canonical homomorphisms \(H'' \to H\) and \(H'' \to H'\); then U is the set of \(s \in S\) such that the induced homomorphism \(H''_s \to H_s\) is an isomorphism (resp. such that \(H''_s \to H_s\) and \(H''_s \to H'_s\) are isomorphisms). By virtue of 2.9, it follows that U is open and closed, and that \(H''_U \to H_U\) (resp. \(H''_U \to H_U\) and \(H''_U \to H'_U\)) are isomorphisms. Whence the desired conclusion.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.11

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, H a subgroup of multiplicative type of G, and \(K = G/H\) (which is a group of multiplicative type, a quotient of G).

  1. (i)

    Suppose G trivial, i.e. diagonalizable. Then there exists a partition of S into open and closed parts \(S_i\), such that for every i, \(H_{S_i}\) and \(K_{S_i}\) are diagonalizable. In particular, if S is connected, H and K are diagonalizable.

  2. (ii)

    Same statement as in (i), replacing "diagonalizable" by "isotrivial", provided S is connected, or locally connected, or quasi-compact.

  3. (iii)

    Suppose G locally trivial (resp. locally isotrivial, resp. quasi-isotrivial); then the same holds for K and for H.

Proof. (i) By hypothesis we have \(G = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\), where M is a finitely generated commutative group. For every quotient group \(M_i\) of M, let \(H_i = \mathbf{D}_S(M_i)\) be the corresponding diagonalizable subgroup of G (VIII 3.1). Let \(S_i\) be the set of \(s \in S\) such that \(H_s = (H_i)_s\); by virtue of 2.10, \(S_i\) is open and closed, and we have \(H_{S_i} = (H_i)_{S_i}\), hence \(H_{S_i}\) is diagonalizable, hence so is \(K_{S_i}\) (VIII 3.1). Evidently the \(S_i\) are pairwise disjoint; I claim that they cover S. This results from the fact that for every s, \(H_s\) is diagonalizable, as a subgroup of the diagonalizable group \(G_s\) (cf. 8.1 below6), hence \(H_s\) is of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{\kappa(s)}(M_i)\), by VIII 1.5 and 3.2 b). Restricting to the family of nonempty \(S_i\), conclusion (i) appears.

(ii) By hypothesis, there exists \(S' \to S\) finite étale surjective such that \(G_{S'}\) is diagonalizable. Hence every point of S′ has a neighborhood U′, both open and closed, such that \(H_{U'}\) and \(K_{U'}\) are diagonalizable. Then the image U of U′ in S is both open and closed, and \(S' \to S\) again induces a finite étale surjective morphism \(U' \to U\), so we see that every point s of S has an open and closed neighborhood U such that \(H_U\) and \(K_U\) are isotrivial. Conclusion (ii) results at once.

(iii) Results at once from (i) and (ii) and the definitions. Note moreover that the "quasi-isotrivial" case will also result from the more general fact that "finite type \(\Rightarrow\) quasi-isotrivial", announced in 1.2 (cf. X 4.5).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: We have added this definition, which appears in Propositions 2.3 and 2.7.
N.D.E.: cf. also V, 9.1 or EGA IV2, 2.7.1.
N.D.E.: and even a closed immersion.
N.D.E.: We have corrected the erroneous reference to IV 4.7.5. Note that Section 8 of the present exposé is independent of Sections 3 to 7.

pages.title: 3. Infinitesimal properties: lifting and conjugation theorems

blocks.html: 3. Infinitesimal properties: lifting and conjugation theorems
The fundamental infinitesimal properties of groups of multiplicative type flow from the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

Let S be an affine scheme, H an S-group of multiplicative type, \(\mathscr{F}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf on S, on which H acts (I 4.7). Then we have \[ \mathrm{H}^i(H, \mathscr{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for} \quad i > 0, \] where \(\mathrm{H}^i\) is the Hochschild cohomology studied in Exp. I, §5.

Indeed, by loc. cit., 5.3, the Hochschild cohomology is made explicit, in terms of the affine rings A of S and B of G, and of the module M over A defining \(\mathscr{F}\), as the cohomology of a complex of A-modules \(\mathrm{C}^{\bullet}(H,M)\), whose formation commutes with every base change \(A \to A'\). Consequently, for a base change \(A \to A'\) with A′ flat over A, we shall have \[ \mathrm{H}^i(H', \mathscr{F}') = \mathrm{H}^i(H,\mathscr{F}) \otimes_A A', \] and consequently, if we suppose A′ even faithfully flat over A, to prove that \(\mathrm{H}^i(H,\mathscr{F})\) is zero, it suffices to prove that this is so for \(\mathrm{H}^i(H',\mathscr{F}')\). This remark reduces us to verifying 3.1 in the case where G is diagonalizable; in that case this was proved in I 5.3.3.
Using the results of Exposé III, we are going to draw from 3.1 various consequences of a geometric nature:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2

Let S be an affine scheme, \(S_0\) an affine subscheme defined by an ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), H a group of multiplicative type over S, G an arbitrary group prescheme over S, \(u, v : H \to G\) two group homomorphisms, \(u_0, v_0 : H_0 \to G_0\) the homomorphisms deduced from u, v by the base change \(S_0 \to S\). If \(\mathscr{J}^2 = 0\) and \(u_0 = v_0\), then there exists a \(g \in G(S)\) such that \(v = \mathrm{int}(g) \circ u\) and \(g_0 = e\).

This results from III 2.1 (ii), and from 3.1 (vanishing of \(\mathrm{H}^1\)).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Under the preliminary conditions of 3.2, suppose moreover G smooth over S, but on the other hand suppose only that \(\mathscr{J}\) is nilpotent (not necessarily of square zero). If there exists \(g_0 \in G_0(S_0)\) such that \(v_0 = \mathrm{int}(g_0) \circ u_0\), then \(g_0\) lifts to a \(g \in G(S)\) such that \(v = \mathrm{int}(g) \circ u\).

By induction on the integer n such that \(\mathscr{J}^n = 0\), we are reduced to the case where \(\mathscr{J}\) has square zero. Moreover, G being smooth over S, we can lift \(g_0\) to an element \(g'\) of G(S). Replacing v by \(v' = \mathrm{int}(g') \circ u\) if necessary, we are then reduced to the situation of 3.2.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Under the preliminary conditions of 3.2, with \(\mathscr{J}\) nilpotent, suppose moreover u central (for example G commutative). Then \(u_0 = v_0\) implies \(u = v\).

Indeed, the reduction to the case \(\mathscr{J}\) of square zero is again immediate, and then it suffices to apply 3.2. In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let S, \(S_0\), H, G be as in 3.2, with \(\mathscr{J}\) nilpotent. Let \(u : H \to G\) be a homomorphism of S-groups such that \(u_0 : H_0 \to G_0\) is the unit homomorphism; then u is the unit homomorphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.6

Let S be an affine scheme, \(S_0\) a subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), H a group of multiplicative type over S, G a group prescheme smooth over S, \(u_0 : H_0 \to G_0\) a homomorphism of the \(S_0\)-groups deduced by the base change \(S_0 \to S\).

Then there exists a homomorphism \(u : H \to G\) of S-groups lifting \(u_0\) (and by 3.3 two such liftings u, u′ are conjugate by an element of G(S) reducing to the unit element of \(G(S_0)\)).

This results from III 2.1 and 2.3, and from 3.1 (vanishing of \(\mathrm{H}^2\) for the existence of the lifting of \(u_0\), vanishing of \(\mathrm{H}^1\) for the uniqueness up to conjugation).

One can prove in the same way the following variants of 3.2 to 3.6 (which in fact imply the preceding statements, by applying them to the graph subgroups of the homomorphisms considered in 3.2 to 3.6):

blocks.label: 3.2 bis
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2 bis

Let S be an affine scheme, \(S_0\) a subscheme defined by an ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), G an S-group prescheme, H, H′ sub-group preschemes of multiplicative type, \(G_0, H_0, H'_0\) the groups over \(S_0\) deduced by the base change \(S_0 \to S\). If \(\mathscr{J}^2 = 0\) and \(H_0 = H'_0\), then there exists \(g \in G(S)\) such that \(H' = \mathrm{int}(g)(H)\) and \(g_0 = e\).

blocks.label: 3.3 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3 bis

Under the preliminary conditions of 3.2 bis, suppose moreover G smooth over S, and \(\mathscr{J}\) nilpotent (not necessarily of square zero). Let \(g_0 \in G_0(S_0)\) be such that \(H'_0 = \mathrm{int}(g_0)(H_0)\); then \(g_0\) lifts to \(g \in G(S)\) such that \(H' = \mathrm{int}(g)(H)\).

blocks.label: 3.4 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4 bis

Under the preliminary conditions of 3.2 bis, suppose \(\mathscr{J}\) nilpotent, H central (for example G commutative). Then \(H_0 = H'_0\) implies \(H = H'\).

blocks.label: 3.6 bis
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.6 bis

Let S be an affine scheme, \(S_0\) a subscheme defined by a nilpotent ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), G a smooth S-group prescheme, \(H_0\) a sub-group prescheme of \(G_0 = G \times_S S_0\), of multiplicative type. Then:

  1. (a)

    There exists a sub-group prescheme H of G, flat over S, such that \(H \times_S S_0 = H_0\).

  2. (b)

    Such an H is necessarily of multiplicative type.

  3. (c)

    Finally, by 3.2 bis, two such liftings H, H′ of \(H_0\) are conjugate by an element \(g \in G(S)\) reducing to the unit element of \(G(S_0)\).

Let us show how one can prove 3.2 bis (of which 3.3 bis and 3.4 bis are an immediate consequence) and assertion (a) of 3.6 bis. The latter results from 3.1 (vanishing of \(\mathrm{H}^2\)) and from III 4.37 (ii). Likewise 3.2 bis results from 3.1 (vanishing of \(\mathrm{H}^1\)) and from III 4.37 (i), at least when G and H are smooth over S.

But using the results of the next exposé, one can very simply deduce 3.2 bis and 3.6 bis, in the general form stated here, from 3.2 resp. 3.6. For 3.2 bis, it suffices indeed to note that by virtue of X 2.1, H and H′ are isomorphic, which brings us back to 3.2.

For 3.6 bis, we note that \(H_0\) is necessarily of finite type over \(S_0\): 7 indeed, as a subprescheme of \(G_0\), which is smooth over \(S_0\), \(H_0\) is locally of finite type over \(S_0\); now, by 2.1 a), \(H_0\) is affine over \(S_0\), hence is of finite type over \(S_0\). Then, by X 4.5, \(H_0\) is quasi-isotrivial, hence comes, by X 2.1, from a group of multiplicative type H over S.8 Then, by 3.6, there exists a homomorphism of S-groups \(u : H \to G\) lifting the immersion \(H_0 \hookrightarrow G_0\); since H and \(H_0\) (resp. G and \(G_0\)) have the same underlying topological space, and since, for every \(h \in H\), the morphism \(\mathscr{O}_{G,u(h)} \to \mathscr{O}_{H,h}\) is surjective (for it is so after reduction modulo \(\mathscr{J}\), which is nilpotent), u is likewise an immersion (cf. EGA I, 4.2.2).

Finally, for every lifting H of \(H_0\) to a flat subgroup of G, H is necessarily of multiplicative type by X 2.39, which also proves assertion (b) of 3.6 bis. (The reader will verify that the results 3.2 bis to 3.6 bis are not used in Exposé X, so that there is no vicious circle!).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.8

10 Let S be a prescheme, \(S_0\) a closed subprescheme defined by a locally nilpotent ideal \(\mathscr{J}\), G an S-group of multiplicative type, X an S-prescheme locally of finite presentation. Suppose that G acts on X, in such a way that \(G_0 = G \times_S S_0\) acts trivially on \(X_0 = X \times_S S_0\). Under these conditions, G acts trivially on X.

The proof is that of III 2.4 b); one can also reduce to loc. cit. by noting that 3.8 reduces at once to the case where G is diagonalizable, which is the case considered in loc. cit.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.9

Let S, \(S_0\) be as above, and \(u : G \to H\) a homomorphism of S-groups, with G of multiplicative type and H locally of finite presentation over S. Suppose that the homomorphism \(u_0 : G_0 \to H_0\) deduced by the base change \(S_0 \to S\) is central. Then u is central.

Indeed, it suffices to apply 3.8 with \(X = H\), letting G act on H by \((g,h) \mapsto \mathrm{int}(u(g)) \cdot h = u(g)\, h\, u(g)^{-1}\).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: The original said: "by 2.1 b), for its fibers are". The editors did not understand this argument, and have substituted for it the one that follows.
N.D.E.: We have expanded the sentence that follows.
N.D.E.: Note that X 2.3 depends in an essential way on Theorem 3.6 of the present exposé.
N.D.E.: We have kept the numbering of the original: there is no Section 3.7.

blocks.html: 4. The density theorem

This theorem (cf. 4.7 below)*, together with the theorem11 of Section 7, will be the essential tool in the present exposé and the two following ones, for passing from the infinitesimal properties of groups of multiplicative type, which we have just developed, to the "finite" properties.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1

Let X be a prescheme. We say that a family \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) of subpreschemes of X is schematically dense if for every open set U of X, and every closed subprescheme U′ of U which contains the \(Z_i \cap U\), we have \(U' = U\).

We say that a subprescheme Z of X is schematically dense in X if this is so for the family reduced to Z.

One sees immediately (cf. EGA IV3, 11.10.1) that the definition is equivalent to saying that for every open set U of X, every section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{O}_U\) which vanishes on the \(Z_i \cap U\) is zero, which also means that the intersection of the kernels of the canonical homomorphisms \(u_i : \mathscr{O}_X \to (v_i)_*(\mathscr{O}_{Z_i})\) is zero, where \(v_i : Z_i \to X\) is the canonical immersion.12

When X lies over a prescheme S, this is again equivalent to saying that for every open set U of X and every pair \((u,v)\) of morphisms from U to an S-prescheme Y separated over S, which coincide on the \(Z_i \cap U\), we have \(u = v\). (Indeed, the relation \(u = v\) is equivalent to the relation \(U' = U\), where U′ is the inverse image of the diagonal of \(Y \times_S Y\) under the S-morphism \(X \to Y \times_S Y\) defined by \((u,v)\); this diagonal is a closed subprescheme of \(Y \times_S Y\), so U′ is a closed subprescheme of U, containing the \(Z_i \cap U\) by the hypothesis on \((u,v)\), so if the family \((Z_i)\) is schematically dense, we shall have \(U' = U\), whence \(u = v\); one sees the converse implication by simply taking \(Y = \mathrm{Spec}\, \mathscr{O}_S[T]\)).
With the terminology introduced in EGA I, end of Section 9.5, to say that the subprescheme Z of X is schematically dense also means that X is identical to the closure subprescheme of Z in X.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2

Let X be a flat S-prescheme, \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of subpreschemes of X, flat over S. Let \(S_0\) be a subprescheme of S, defined by a nilpotent ideal \(\mathscr{J}\); we suppose the modules \(\mathscr{J}^n/\mathscr{J}^{n+1}\) locally free over \(S_0\).

Let \(X_0, Z_{i,0}\) be deduced from \(X, Z_i\) by the base change \(S_0 \to S\). Then, if the family \((Z_{i,0})_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in \(X_0\), the family \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in X.

Suppose \(\mathscr{J}^{n+1} = 0\) (where \(n \geqslant 0\)); let us argue by induction on n, the assertion being trivial for \(n = 0\). Defining the \(S_m, X_m, Z_{i,m}\) by reduction modulo \(\mathscr{J}^{m+1}\) as usual, the induction hypothesis already implies that \((Z_{i,n-1})_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in \(X_{n-1}\). Replacing X by an induced open subscheme if necessary, we are reduced to proving that every section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) which vanishes on the \(Z_i\) is zero.

Now the section \(f_{n-1}\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{n-1}} = \mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{J}^n\mathscr{O}_X\) defined by \(f\) vanishes on the \(Z_{i,n-1}\), hence is zero, hence \(f\) is a section of \(\mathscr{J}^n \mathscr{O}_X\). Since X is flat over S, we have \[ \mathscr{J}^n \mathscr{O}_X \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } E \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} \mathscr{O}_{X_0}, \qquad \text{where } E = \mathscr{J}^n = \mathscr{J}^n/\mathscr{J}^{n+1}. \] Likewise, since each \(Z_i\) is flat over S, the restriction \(f_i\) of \(f\) to \(Z_i\) may be regarded as a section of: \[ \mathscr{J}^n \mathscr{O}_{Z_i} \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } E \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} \mathscr{O}_{Z_{i,0}}. \] By hypothesis, the \(f_i\) are zero. Now, E is locally free by hypothesis, hence so is \(F = E \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S_0}} \mathscr{O}_{X_0}\). Hence \(f\) is a section of the locally free module F on \(X_0\), such that for every i its restriction to \(Z_{i,0}\) is zero. Since \((Z_{i,0})_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in \(X_0\), it follows at once that \(f\) is zero. Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Let X be an S-prescheme locally noetherian and flat over S, \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of subpreschemes of X flat over S. Suppose that for every \(s \in S\), the family \((Z_{i,s})_{i \in I}\) of fibers at s is schematically dense in \(X_s\). Then the family \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in X.

Replacing X by an induced open subscheme if necessary, we are reduced to proving that every section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) whose restriction to the \(Z_i\) is zero, is zero.13 For this, it suffices to prove that for every \(x \in X\), the image of \(f\) in \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is zero. Denote by \(\mathfrak{m}_x\) the maximal ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), and by \(\mathfrak{m}_s\) that of \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\), where s is the image of x in S. Since \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is noetherian, we have \(\bigcap_{n \in \mathbb{N}} \mathfrak{m}_x^n = 0\), whence, a fortiori, \(\bigcap_{n \in \mathbb{N}} \mathscr{O}_{X,x}\mathfrak{m}_s^n = 0\).

Hence, it suffices to show that, for every integer n, the section induced by \(f\) on \(X \times_S \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}/\mathfrak{m}_s^{n+1})\) is zero. By hypothesis, the family of fibers \(Z_{i,s} = Z_i \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S,s}} \kappa(s)\) is schematically dense in the fiber \(X_s = X \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{S,s}} \kappa(s)\). This therefore reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of a local ring whose maximal ideal J is nilpotent, \(S_0 = \mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(s)\), and the hypotheses of 4.2 are satisfied, whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, X an S-prescheme locally noetherian and flat over S, \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of subpreschemes of X flat over S. Suppose that for every \(s \in S\), the family \((Z_{i,s})_{i \in I}\) of fibers at s is schematically dense in \(X_s\).

Then, for every base change \(S' \to S\) (S′ not necessarily locally noetherian), the family \((Z'_{i})_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in X′ (that is, the family \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) is universally schematically dense in X relative to S, cf. EGA IV3, Def. 11.10.8).

Proof.14 Let \(f'\) be a section of \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\) on an open set U′ of X′, which vanishes on the \(Z'_i\); we must show that \(f'\) is zero. Let \(s' \in S'\); it suffices to prove that \(f'\) is zero at all the points of U′ above \(s'\). Let s be the image of \(s'\) in S; replacing S, S′ by the spectra of the local rings at s, \(s'\) if necessary, we may suppose S, S′ local, and that s, \(s'\) are their closed points. We may moreover suppose X affine, so \[ S = \mathrm{Spec}(A), \qquad S' = \mathrm{Spec}(A'), \qquad X = \mathrm{Spec}(B), \qquad X' = \mathrm{Spec}(B'), \qquad B' = B \otimes_A A' \] where A, A′ are local, \(A \to A'\) is a local homomorphism, and A, B are noetherian. We may also suppose U′ affine, of the form \(X'_{g'}\), with \(g' \in B'\). For every sub-A-algebra \(A''\) of A′, we consider \(S'' = \mathrm{Spec}(A'')\) and \(X'', Z^{i\prime\prime}_n\) deduced from X, \(Z^i\) by the base change \(S'' \to S\), whence the diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{ Z^i \ar[d] & Z_n^{i\prime\prime} \ar[l] \ar[d] & Z_n^{i\prime} \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ X \ar[d] & X'' \ar[l] \ar[d] & X' \ar[l] \ar[d] \\ S & S'' \ar[l] & S' \ar[l] } \]

We shall restrict ourselves in what follows to the A″ which are localizations of finitely generated sub-A-algebras \(A''_1\) of A′ at \(\mathfrak{m}' \cap A''_1\) (where \(\mathfrak{m}'\) is the maximal ideal of A′), so that the homomorphisms \(A \to A'' \to A'\) are local. Note that these A″ form an increasing filtered family of subalgebras whose union is A′, hence their inductive limit is likewise A′. We therefore also have \(B' = \varinjlim_{A''} B \otimes_A A''\). Hence, there exist an A″ and an element \(g'' \in B'' = B \otimes_A A''\), whose image in B′ is \(g'\).15

By Lemma 4.5 below, the property that the family of fibers \((Z_{i,s})_{i \in I}\) be schematically dense in \(X_s\) is preserved under every base change \(S'' \to S\); consequently, since each S″ is locally noetherian, if we replace S by an appropriate S″, and X, \(Z_i\) by \(X'', Z''_i\), the hypotheses of 4.4 will be preserved.

Hence, replacing S by S″ (and X, \(Z_i\) by \(X'', Z''_i\)) if necessary, we may suppose that \(g'\) comes from \(g \in B\). Replacing X by the open set \(X_g = U\) if necessary, we may therefore suppose that \(U' = X'\). One sees likewise that we may suppose that \(f'\) comes from a section \(f\) of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) on X.

Let \(Y = V(f)\) be the subscheme of X defined by \(f\), and \(Y_i = Z_i \times_X Y\) its intersection with \(Z_i\), which is a closed subscheme of \(Z_i\), equal to \(V(f_i)\), where \(f_i\) is the section of \(\mathscr{O}_{Z_i}\) induced by \(f\). Denoting by \(Y', Y'_i\) the S′-schemes deduced from the preceding ones by base change, we shall again have \[ Y' = V(f'), \qquad Y'_i = Z'_i \times_{X'} Y' = V(f'_i), \] and we have the analogous relations for \(Y'', Y''_i\). The hypothesis that \(f'\) vanishes on the \(Z'_i\) is expressed by the relations \(Y'_i = Z'_i\) for every i.16

Let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the maximal ideal of A. For every integer \(n \geqslant 0\), let us introduce the subscheme \(S_n = \mathrm{Spec}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\) of S, and the schemes \(X_n, Y_n, Z_{i,n}, Y_{i,n}\) deduced from \(X, Y, Z_i, Y_i\) by the base change \(S_n \to S\). In general, for every S-prescheme P, we shall set \(P_n = P \times_S S_n\). For every n, and \(i \in I\), consider the functor \[ \mathrm{F}^i_n = \prod_{Z^i_n/S_n} Y^i_n/Z^i_n \ : \ (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S_n} \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Ens}) \]

defined by (cf. VIII, 6.4): for every P over \(S_n\), \[ \mathrm{F}^i_n(P) = \Gamma\big((Y^i_n)_P/(Z^i_n)_P\big) = \begin{cases} \varnothing & \text{if } (Y^i_n)_P \neq (Z^i_n)_P; \\ \{\mathrm{id}\} & \text{if } (Y^i_n)_P = (Z^i_n)_P. \end{cases} \]

Since \(S_n\) is local artinian, and \(Z^i_n\) flat hence essentially free over \(S_n\), then, by VIII, 6.4, each \(\mathrm{F}^i_n\) is representable by a closed subprescheme \(\mathrm{T}^i_n\) of \(S_n\).17 The completion \(\widehat{A}\) of the local ring A is noetherian since A is, and it is the projective limit of the \(A_n\). Denote by \(\mathrm{K}^i_n\) the ideal of \(A_n = A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\) defining \(\mathrm{T}^i_n\), and by \(\mathrm{K}^i\) the projective limit of the \(\mathrm{K}^i_n\); it is an ideal of \(\widehat{A}\).

For fixed i, \(m \geqslant n\) and every \(S_n\)-prescheme P, we have \[ (Y^i_n)_P = Y^i \times_S S_n \times_{S_n} P = Y^i \times_S P = (Y^i_m)_P, \] and likewise \((Z^i_n)_P = (Z^i_m)_P\); it follows that \(\mathrm{F}^i_n\) is the restriction \(\mathrm{F}^i_m \times_{S_m} S_n\) of \(\mathrm{F}^i_m\) to \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S_n}\), whence \(\mathrm{T}^i_n = \mathrm{T}^i_m \times_{S_m} S_n\). We therefore have a commutative diagram with exact rows: \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm{K}^i_m \ar[r] \ar@{->>}[d] & A_m \ar[r] \ar@{->>}[d] & \mathscr{O}(\mathrm{T}^i_m) \ar[r] \ar@{->>}[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathrm{K}^i_n \ar[r] & A_n \ar[r] & \mathscr{O}(\mathrm{T}^i_n) \ar[r] & 0 } \]

where all the vertical arrows are surjective. This has the following consequences: on the one hand, the projective system \((\mathscr{O}(\mathrm{T}^i_n))_{n \in \mathbb{N}}\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition, and hence the projective limit of the \(\mathscr{O}(\mathrm{T}^i_n)\) is identified with the topological quotient ring \(\widehat{A}/\mathrm{K}^i\) (cf. EGA 0III, §13.2). On the other hand, the map \(\mathrm{K}^i \to \mathrm{K}^i_n\) is surjective (cf. [BEns], III, §7.4, Prop. 5), whence it follows that \(\mathrm{K}^i_n \simeq (\mathrm{K}^i + \mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\widehat{A})/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\widehat{A}\) and \(\mathscr{O}(\mathrm{T}^i_n) \simeq (\widehat{A}/\mathrm{K})\otimes_A (A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\).

In other words, \((\mathrm{T}^i_n)_n\) is an inductive system of artinian affine schemes, and the inductive limit \(\mathrm{T}^i = \varinjlim_n \mathrm{T}^i_n\) is a closed formal subscheme of the formal scheme \(\widehat{S} = \mathrm{Spf}(\widehat{A})\) (cf. EGA I, §10), whose reduction modulo \(\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\) is \(\mathrm{T}^i_n\).

Let T be the closed formal subscheme of \(\widehat{S}\) which is the intersection of the \(\mathrm{T}^i\), that is, defined by \(\mathrm{K} = \sum_i \mathrm{K}^i\). Since \(\widehat{A}\) is noetherian, there exists a finite subset J of I such that \(\mathrm{K} = \sum_{i \in J} \mathrm{K}^i\) (i.e. \(\mathrm{T} = \bigcap_{i \in J} \mathrm{T}^i\)). Note then that for every n, we have \(\mathrm{K}_n = \sum_{i \in J} \mathrm{K}^i_n\), where \(\mathrm{K}_n\) denotes the image of K in \(A_n\).

Recall that \(f^i\) denotes the image of \(f \in B = \mathscr{O}(X)\) in \(\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\), and \(f^{i\prime}\) its image in \(\mathscr{O}(Z^{i\prime}_n) = \mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_A A'\); the hypothesis \(Y^{i\prime} = Z^{i\prime}\) is equivalent to the vanishing of \(f^{i\prime}\). Since \(\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_A A'\) is the inductive limit of the subalgebras \(\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_A A''\) (where A″ satisfies the conditions made explicit above), there therefore exists an A″ such that \(Y^{i\prime\prime} = Z^{i\prime\prime}\). A priori, A″ depends on i, but one can find an A″ which works for all \(i \in J\), since J is finite. Set \(S'' = \mathrm{Spec}(A'')\) and \(S''_{(n)} = S'' \times_S S_n\).18

Since \(Y^{i\prime\prime} \times_{S''} S''_{(n)} = (Y^i_n)_{S''_{(n)}}\) equals \(Z^{i\prime\prime} \times_{S''} S''_{(n)} = (Z^i_n)_{S''_{(n)}}\) for every \(i \in J\), it results from the definition of the \(\mathrm{T}^i_n\) that \(S''_{(n)} \to S_n\) factors through \(\mathrm{T}^i_n\) for every \(i \in J\), hence also through \(\mathrm{T}_n = \bigcap_{i \in J} \mathrm{T}^i_n\), hence also through \(\mathrm{T}^i_n\) for every \(i \in I\). Denoting by \(f''\) the image of \(f\) in \(B'' = B \otimes_A A''\), and by \(f''_{(n)}\) its image in \(B''_{(n)} = B''/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}B''\), this means that for every n, \(f''_{(n)}\) vanishes on the \(Z^i \times_S S''_{(n)}\).

Since the morphisms \(A \to A'' \to A'\) are local, the image \(s''\) of \(s'\) in S″ is the closed point of S″, corresponding to the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}''\) of B″, and the image of \(s''\) in S is s. Fix n and denote now by \(S''_n\) the n-th infinitesimal neighborhood of \(s''\) in S″, that is, \(\mathrm{Spec}(A''/\mathfrak{m}''^{n+1})\). Set \(B''_n = B''/\mathfrak{m}''^{n+1}B''\) and \(Z^{i\prime\prime}_n = Z^i \times_S S''_n = \mathrm{Spec}(B''_n)\). Then the image \(f''_n\) of \(f''_{(n)}\) in \(B''_n\) vanishes on \(Z^{i\prime\prime}_n\), for every i. Now, by Lemma 4.5 below, the family of fibers \[ Z^{i\prime\prime}_0 = Z^{i\prime\prime}_n \times_{S''_n} \kappa(s'') = Z^i_s \times_{\kappa(s)} \kappa(s'') \] is schematically dense in the fiber \(X''_0 = X''_n \times_{S''_n} \kappa(s'') = X_s \times_{\kappa(s)} \kappa(s'')\). Hence \(S''_n, X''_n, (Z^{i\prime\prime}_n)_{i \in I}\), and \(S''_0 = \mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(s'')\) satisfy the hypotheses of Lemma 4.2; it therefore follows that \(f''_n = 0\), i.e. \(f'' \in \mathfrak{m}''^{n+1}B''\), and this for every n.

Since B is noetherian and \(B'' = B \otimes_A A''\) is a localization of a finitely generated B-algebra, B″ is noetherian, hence its local rings are separated for their usual topology. It follows that \(f''\) is zero at the points \(x''\) of X″ such that \(\mathfrak{m}''B''\) is contained in the maximal ideal of \(\mathscr{O}_{X'',x''}\), i.e. at the points of X″ above \(s''\). A fortiori, \(f'\) is zero at the points \(x'\) of X′ above \(s'\). Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5.0

19 Let X be an S-prescheme, \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of subpreschemes of X, and \(S' \to S\) a faithfully flat morphism. If the family \((Z'_i)_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in X′, then \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in X.

This results from EGA IV3, 11.10.5 (i) and 11.9.10 (i). It remains to postpone the proof of the following

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5

Let X be a locally noetherian prescheme20 over a field k, \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of subpreschemes of X, k′ an extension of k, X′ and \(Z'_i\) the preschemes deduced from X and \(Z_i\) by the base change \(k'/k\). For \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) to be schematically dense in X, it is necessary and sufficient that \((Z'_i)_{i \in I}\) be so in X′.

The "sufficient" part follows from 4.5.0; let us prove the "necessary" part.21 First, we may suppose \(X = \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) affine. For every i, let \((Z^{ij})_{j \in \mathrm{J}_i}\) be a covering of \(Z_i\) by affine open sets; replacing \((Z_i)_{i \in I}\) by the family \((Z^{ij})_{(i,j) \in J}\), where \(J = \coprod_{i \in I} J_i\), we may also suppose the \(Z_i\) affine.

Let \(g \in B' = B \otimes_k k'\) and let \(t' \in B'_g\) be a section of \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\) on the affine open set \(U' = X'_g\), vanishing on the \(Z'_i \cap U'\), i.e. whose image in each \((\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_k k')_g\) is zero. There exists a finitely generated sub-k-algebra A of k′ such that \(g \in B_A = B \otimes_k A\) and \(t' \in (B_A)_g\). The map \(\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_k A \to \mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_k k'\) being injective, so is the map \[ (\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_k A)_g \longrightarrow (\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_k k')_g\,; \] taking the hypothesis into account, this implies that the image of \(t'\) in each \((\mathscr{O}(Z^i)\otimes_k A)_g\) is zero. This reduces us to showing that the family \((Z^i_A)_{i \in I}\) is schematically dense in \(X_A\),22 which results from EGA IV3, 11.9.10 b).

Let us note in passing the following result, which will be used in a later exposé:23

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6

Let X be a flat S-prescheme, U an open subset of X. Suppose that for every \(s \in S\), \(U_s\) is schematically dense in \(X_s\), and that X is locally noetherian, or locally of finite presentation over S. Then U is schematically dense in X.

Suppose for simplicity that U is retrocompact in X (cf. EGA IV3, 11.10.10 and 11.9.17 for the general case). The locally noetherian case for X is included only for the record; it is contained in 4.3.

In the second case considered, we may evidently suppose S and X affine; then X and U are of finite presentation over \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\). The ring A is the inductive limit of its finitely generated sub-\(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras \(A_i\). The "patented procedure" already used (cf. EGA IV3, 8.8.2 and 8.10.5 (iii)) shows that there exist an i, an affine scheme \(X_i\) over \(S_i = \mathrm{Spec}(A_i)\) and an open set \(U_i\) in \(X_i\), from which X, U are deduced by the base change \(S \to S_i\). Let \(E_i\) be the subset of \(S_i\) consisting of the \(s \in S_i\) such that \((U_i)_s\) is schematically dense in \((X_i)_s\).

By EGA IV2, 5.9.9 and 5.10.2, \(E_i\) is the set of \(s \in S_i\) such that \((U_i)_s\) contains the set \(\mathrm{Ass}\, \mathscr{O}_{(X_i)_s}\) of points "associated" with the structure sheaf of \((X_i)_s\), and by EGA IV3, 11.9.17.i this condition is of constructible nature, i.e. \(E_i\) is a constructible subset of \(S_i\).24

By 4.5, the inverse image of \(E_i\) under \(S_j \to S_i\) (resp. under \(S \to S_i\)) is \(E_j\) (resp. the set E of \(s \in S\) such that \(U_s\) is schematically dense in \(X_s\)). Moreover, by hypothesis, \(E = S\), which is also the inverse image under each \(S \to S_i\) of \(S_i\). By EGA IV3, 8.3.11, this implies that there exists a \(j \geqslant i\) such that \(E_j = S_j\), i.e. such that for every \(s \in S_j\), \((U_j)_s\) is schematically dense in \((X_j)_s\).

Then, by virtue of 4.4 applied to \((S_j, X_j, U_j)\) and to the base change \(S \to S_j\), it follows that U is schematically dense in X. Note moreover that 4.4 is used here only in the case of a family whose index set is finite (in fact, reduced to one element), in which case the proof of 4.4 simplifies considerably, as the reader will verify for himself.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.7

Let S be a prescheme, G a group of multiplicative type and of finite type over S. For every integer \(n > 0\), let \({}_n G\) be the kernel of \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_G\). Then the family of subpreschemes \(({}_n G)_{n > 0}\) of G is schematically dense (Def. 4.1).

We distinguish two cases.

a) Case S locally noetherian. Then by 4.3 we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field k. By 4.5.0, we may suppose k algebraically closed and G diagonalizable, i.e. of the form \(\mathbf{D}_k(M)\), where M is a finitely generated commutative group. Then M is of the form \(\Gamma \times \mathbb{Z}^r\), with \(\Gamma\) finite, hence G is of the form \(G_1 \times G_2\), with \(G_1 = \mathbf{D}(\Gamma)\) and \(G_2 = \mathbb{G}_m^r\), and for n multiplicatively large (namely n a multiple of the order of \(\Gamma\)) we shall have \({}_n G = G_1 \times {}_n G_2\), since we shall have \({}_n G_1 = G_1\).

Applying 4.3 again to the projection \(G \to G_1\), we are reduced to the case of \(G_2\), i.e. to the case where \(G = \mathbb{G}_m^r\). Since G is then reduced, it comes to the same thing to say that \(({}_n G)_{n>0}\) is schematically dense in G, or that the union of the \({}_n G\) is dense in G for the usual topology. Since \({}_n G = ({}_n \mathbb{G}_m)^r\), we are reduced to the case \(G = \mathbb{G}_m\), so G irreducible of dimension 1. Then this results from the fact that the union of the \({}_n G\) (equal to the set of roots of unity in k) is infinite.

b) General case. For every point s of S, there exist an open neighborhood U of s and a faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism \(S' \to U\) such that \(G' = G_{S'}\) is diagonalizable, i.e. of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{S'}(M)\). By 4.5.0 again, we can reduce to the case of G′, so we may suppose G diagonalizable; hence it comes from the absolute group \(H = \mathbf{D}_{\mathbb{Z}}(M)\) over \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})\) by the base change \(S \to \mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})\). By the proof of a), for every \(s \in \mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})\), the family \(({}_n H_s)_{n>0}\) is schematically dense in \(H\); it now suffices to apply 4.4.25

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8

a) Let \(u, v : H \to G\) be two homomorphisms of S-group preschemes, with H of multiplicative type and26 of finite type, and suppose that for every integer \(n > 0\), the restrictions of u and v to \({}_n H\) are identical. Then \(u = v\).

b) Let \(H_1, H_2\) be two groups of multiplicative type and of finite type of a group prescheme G, and suppose that for every integer \(n > 0\), we have \({}_n H_1 = {}_n H_2\); then \(H_1 = H_2\).

The first assertion results from the second, by considering the subgroups \(H_1\) and \(H_2\) of \(H \times G\), graphs of u and v. To prove b), let \(H = H_1 \cap H_2\); it is a sub-group prescheme of \(H_i\) \((i=1,2)\), and we must show that it is identical to \(H_i\); now the hypothesis means that it contains the \({}_n H_i\). We are therefore reduced to proving the following

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9

Let G be an S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type, and H a sub-group prescheme which contains the \({}_n G\), \(n > 0\). Then \(H = G\).

By virtue of 4.7 we are reduced to proving that the subprescheme H is closed, or again that it equals G set-theoretically. This reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of a field, but then every sub-group prescheme of G is closed (VIB 1.4.2), whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.10

Under the conditions of 4.7, let m be an integer \(> 0\) which has the following properties: for every \(s \in S\), m is not a power of the characteristic of \(\kappa(s)\), and if \(G_s\) is of type M, the prime divisors of the torsion of M divide m. (N.B. this second condition is always satisfied if G is a torus). Then the proof given shows that in the statement of 4.7 and in Corollaries 4.8 and 4.9, one may confine oneself to considering the subgroups of the form \({}_{(m^r)}G\), with \(r > 0\).

Moreover, one sees at once that when G is smooth over S, then for every point \(s \in S\), there exist an open neighborhood U of s and an integer \(m > 0\), prime to all the residue characteristics of U, which satisfies the preceding conditions for \(G_U\). (Take for example for m the order of the torsion of the type of G at s, cf. 1.4.1 a) and 2.1 e).) Under these conditions, one finds \({}_{(m^r)}G\) which are finite and étale over S, and whose family is schematically dense, provided one restricts to lying above U. This remark makes it possible in certain cases (notably those involving Theorems 3.2 and 3.2 bis, cf. XI 6, but not those involving Theorems 3.6 and 3.6 bis) to dispense with Theorem 3.1, which involves Hochschild cohomology.

pages.title: 5. Central homomorphisms of groups of multiplicative type

blocks.html: 5. Central homomorphisms of groups of multiplicative type

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.0

27 Let \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) be a noetherian local ring, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), and H a scheme finite over S, so \(H = \mathrm{Spec}(B)\), where B is finite over A. Let K be a subscheme of H such that by reduction modulo \(\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\) we have \(K_n = H_n\) for every n. Then \(K = H\).

Let s be the closed point of S. Note first that K is a closed subscheme of H. Indeed, it is a priori a closed subscheme of an open set U of H. But K, hence U, contains the fiber \(K_s = H_s\); since the morphism \(H \to S\) is finite, hence closed, it follows that the complement of U is empty, i.e. \(U = H\). Hence K is defined by an ideal I of B. The hypothesis implies that I is contained in \(\mathfrak{m}^n B\) for every n; since B is a finite A-module, it is separated for the \(\mathfrak{m}\)-adic topology, whence \(I = 0\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

Let \(u, v : H \to G\) be two homomorphisms of S-group preschemes, with H of multiplicative type and of finite type, and S locally noetherian or G of finite presentation over S. Let \(s \in S\) be such that \(u_s = v_s\), and suppose \(u_s\) central. Then there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(u_U = v_U\).

Let us distinguish the two cases:

a) S locally noetherian.28 Let \(K = \mathrm{Ker}(u,v)\) be the inverse image of the diagonal of \(G \times_S G\) under the morphism \((u,v)\); it is a sub-group prescheme of H. We want to find U such that \(K_U = H_U\). Note that, since S is locally noetherian and H of finite type over S, H is locally noetherian, hence the immersion \(K \hookrightarrow H\) is of finite type (cf. EGA I, 6.3.5). Hence K is of finite type over S, hence of finite presentation over S, since S is locally noetherian. Consequently, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2.4, to show that there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(K_U = H_U\), it suffices to show that \(K_{S'} = H_{S'}\), where S′ is the spectrum of \(A = \mathscr{O}_{S,s}\). We may therefore suppose S local with closed point s. Replacing the noetherian local ring A by its completion \(\widehat{A}\) if necessary, which introduces a faithfully flat and quasi-compact base change \(\widehat{S} \to S\), we may even suppose29, if we wish, A complete.

By virtue of 3.4 we have \(u_n = v_n\) for every n, where as usual the index n indicates reduction modulo \(\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\) (\(\mathfrak{m}\) being the maximal ideal of A). For every integer \(m > 0\), denoting by \({}_m u, {}_m v\) the homomorphisms \({}_m H \to G\) induced by u, v, we therefore also have \(({}_m u)_n = ({}_m v)_n\). This being true for every n, and \({}_m H\) being finite over S by virtue of 2.2, it follows that \({}_m u = {}_m v\), by 5.0. This being true for every m, we therefore have \(u = v\) by virtue of 4.7.

b) G of finite presentation.30 Since H is also of finite presentation over S, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2, we may again suppose S local with closed point s and prove that then \(u = v\). If \(f : S' \to S\) is a faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism and if we denote by \(f_H\) the morphism H′ → H and by \(u', v' : H' \to G'\) the homomorphisms deduced from u, v, then the equality \(u' = v'\) implies \(u' \circ f_H = v' \circ f_H\), whence \(u = v\), since \(f_H\) is an epimorphism. Hence, after a faithfully flat and quasi-compact extension of the base if necessary, we may suppose moreover H diagonalizable, hence of the form \(\mathbf{D}_S(M)\), with M a finitely generated commutative group.

Let us introduce, as in the proof of 4.6, the increasing filtered family of finitely generated sub-\(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras \(A_i\) of \(A = \mathscr{O}_{S,s}\), and \(S_i = \mathrm{Spec}(A_i)\). Note that \(H = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\) comes, for every i, from the diagonalizable group \(H_i = \mathbf{D}_{S_i}(M)\). Since G is of finite presentation over S, then, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2 (see also VIB, 10.2 and 10.3), there exist an index i, a group prescheme \(G_i\) of finite presentation over \(S_i\), and morphisms of \(S_i\)-groups \(u_i, v_i : H_i \to G_i\) from which u, v come by base change. Let \(s_i\) be the image of s in \(S_i\) and let \(\rho_i : H_i \times_{S_i} G_i \to G_i\) be the morphism of \(S_i\)-preschemes defined by \(\rho_i(h,g) = u_i(h)\, g\, u_i(h)^{-1}\). Then, since \(u_s\) is central, \(\rho_s = \rho_i \times_{\kappa(s_i)} \kappa(s)\) equals the second projection, hence the same holds for \(\rho_i\) (since \(\kappa(s_i) \to \kappa(s)\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact), i.e. \((u_i)_{s_i}\) is central. Likewise, since \(u_s = v_s\), we have \((u_i)_{s_i} = (v_i)_{s_i}\). We may then apply a) to the situation over \(S_i\), whence at once the announced conclusion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2

Let \(u : H \to G\) be a homomorphism of S-group preschemes, with H of multiplicative type and of finite type, and S locally noetherian or G of finite presentation over S. Let \(s \in S\) and suppose that \(u_s\) is the unit homomorphism. Then there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(u_U\) is the unit homomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3

Let u, H, G be as in 5.2, and suppose moreover G separated over S. Let U be the set of \(s \in S\) such that \(u_s\) is the unit homomorphism. Then U is an open and closed subset of S, and \(u_U : H_U \to G_U\) is the unit homomorphism.

It remains only to prove that U is closed. Now let \(K = \mathrm{Ker}(u)\); since G is separated over S, K is a closed subprescheme of H, and U is the set of \(s \in S\) such that \(K_s = H_s\). One then sees easily that U is closed, for example as an application of VIII 6.4 (H being essentially free over S by VIII 6.3), or by noting that we may suppose S reduced and U dense in S, hence schematically dense in S, which implies \(H_U\) schematically dense in H since H is flat over S31, and since u and the unit homomorphism coincide on \(H_U\), they coincide on H.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

Let S be a prescheme, H and G two S-groups, with H of multiplicative type and of finite type and G separated and of finite presentation over S, \(\pi : S' \to S\) a32 universal effective epimorphism (for example, a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, or a morphism admitting a section, cf. IV 1.12) with geometrically connected fibers.33

Let \(u' : H' \to G'\) be a central homomorphism of the S′-groups deduced from H, G by the base change \(S' \to S\). Then there exists a unique homomorphism \(u : H \to G\) of S-groups such that \(u \times_S S' = u'\). When S′/S admits a section g, then u is the morphism deduced from \(u'\) by the base change \(g : S \to S'\).

Since \(\pi\) is a universal effective epimorphism, so is \(H' \to H\), whence the uniqueness of u, cf. the beginning of the proof of 5.1 b). If \(\pi\) admits a section g, then \(u' = \pi^*(u)\) implies \(u = g^*\pi^*(u) = g^*(u')\).

For the existence of u, we are reduced, by IV 2.3, to showing that the two homomorphisms \(u''_1, u''_2 : H'' \to G''\) of S″-groups deduced from \(u'\) by the two base changes \(\mathrm{pr}_1, \mathrm{pr}_2 : S'' = S' \times_S S' \to S'\), are identical. Now they coincide on the diagonal of S″; more precisely, the inverse images of \(u''_1\) and \(u''_2\) under the diagonal morphism \(S' \to S''\) are identical (being equal to \(u'\)).34 Since \(u''_1\) and \(u''_2\) are central, we may apply 5.3 to the morphism \(u''_1(u''_2)^{-1}\). There exists therefore an open and closed subset U of S″, containing the diagonal of S″, such that \(u''_1\) and \(u''_2\) coincide above U.

Now, the fibers of S′/S being geometrically connected, the same holds for those of S″/S, which are therefore a fortiori connected, whence it results that U (containing the diagonals of the said fibers) contains the said fibers, hence is equal to S″, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Let S be a prescheme, K an S-group prescheme locally of finite type35 and with connected fibers, H a subgroup of multiplicative type and of finite type, normal in K. Then H is a central subgroup of K.

36 Note first that \(\pi : K \to S\) has geometrically connected fibers, since for a group scheme locally of finite type over a field, connected implies geometrically connected (cf. VIA 2.4). We may then apply 5.4, taking \(G = H\) and \(S' = K\), to the homomorphism of K-groups \(u' : H_K \to H_K\) defined set-theoretically by \((h,k) \mapsto (khk^{-1}, k)\), which is central since H is commutative. The inverse image of \(u'\) under the unit section \(\varepsilon : S \to K\) is the identity homomorphism of K, hence by 5.4 the same holds for \(H_K \to H_K\), hence H is central in K.
Let us state the variants of the preceding results for central subgroups of multiplicative type. One obtains, proceeding as for the preceding results (and using 3.2 bis):

blocks.label: 5.1 bis
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1 bis

Let G be an S-group prescheme, \(H_1\) and \(H_2\) two sub-group preschemes of multiplicative type and of finite type, with \((H_1)_s\) central. We suppose S locally noetherian or G of finite presentation over S. Then for every \(s \in S\) such that \((H_1)_s = (H_2)_s\), there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(H_1|_U = H_2|_U\).

blocks.label: 5.3 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3 bis

Under the preceding conditions, suppose moreover G separated over S. Let U be the set of \(s \in S\) such that \((H_1)_s = (H_2)_s\). Then U is an open and closed subset of S, and \(H_1|_U = H_2|_U\).

blocks.label: 5.4 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4 bis

Let S be a prescheme, G a group prescheme separated and of finite presentation over S, \(S' \to S\) a covering morphism for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, with geometrically connected fibers, H′ a subgroup of multiplicative type and of finite type of \(G' = G \times_S S'\).

Then there exists a unique subgroup H of G such that \(H' = H \times_S S'\), and H is of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, by 1.1 and 2.1 b). If \(S' \to S\) admits a section g, then H is the inverse image of the subgroup H′ of G′ under \(g : S \to S'\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.6

Let S be a prescheme, \(u : H \to G\) a homomorphism of S-group preschemes, with H of multiplicative type and of finite type, G of finite presentation over S.

a) Suppose G has connected fibers, and let \(s \in S\) be such that \(u_s : H_s \to G_s\) is a central homomorphism. Then there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that the homomorphism \(H|_U \to G|_U\) induced by u is central.

b) Suppose that for every \(s \in S\), \(u_s : H_s \to G_s\) is central. Then u is central.37

Let us prove (a). Proceeding exactly as in the proof of 5.1 (b), we may suppose S local with closed point s, then \(H = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\) diagonalizable, then that there exist a finitely generated sub-\(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra \(A_i\) of \(A = \mathscr{O}_{S,s}\) and a morphism \(u_i : \mathbf{D}_{S_i}(M) \to G_i\) such that \(u_{s_i}\) is central (where \(s_i\) is the image of s in \(S_i\)) and from which u comes by base change. This reduces us to the case where S is local noetherian, with closed point s, and we must then prove that u is central.

Let K be the subprescheme \(\mathrm{Ker}(v,w)\), where \(v, w : H \times_S G \rightrightarrows G\) are defined by \[ v(h,g) = g, \qquad w(h,g) = \mathrm{int}(u(h)) \cdot g = u(h)\, g\, u(h)^{-1}. \] Then K is a sub-G-group of the G-group \(H_G = H \times_S G\); we want to show that it equals \(H_G\) itself. By virtue of 4.9 we are reduced to proving that it contains the \({}_m(H_G) = ({}_m H) \times_S G\) for every integer \(m > 0\), which reduces us to the case where \(H = {}_m H\), hence H finite over S.

Let e be the unit element of the fiber \(G_s\); then \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{G,e})\) is a noetherian local scheme (G being of finite presentation over S noetherian); set \(S'_n = S' \times_S S_n\), where \(S_n = \mathrm{Spec}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\). Then \(K_{S'} = K \times_G S'\) is a subprescheme of \(H_{S'} = H \times_S S'\) and, by 3.9, we have \(K_{S'_n} = H_{S'_n}\) for every n.38 Since \(H_{S'}\) is finite over S′, we conclude from 5.0 (applied to the noetherian local ring B) that \(K_{S'} = H_{S'}\).

On the other hand, since \(H \times_S G\) is noetherian (being of finite presentation over S noetherian), the immersion \(K \hookrightarrow H \times_S G\) is of finite type (cf. EGA I, 6.3.5), so that K is of finite type, hence of finite presentation over G. Then the equality \(K_{S'} = H_G \times_G S'\) implies, by EGA IV3, 8.8.2.4, that there exists an open neighborhood W of e in G such that \(K \times_G W = H_G \times_G W = H \times_S W\). Hence K contains the open neighborhood \(V = H \times_S W\) of the unit section of \(G_H\) over H.

For every \(t \in H\), the fiber \(G_t\) (being a \(\kappa(t)\)-algebraic group) is Cohen-Macaulay (VIA, 1.1.1), hence without embedded components; since it is moreover connected, hence irreducible (VIA, 2.4), its unique associated point is its generic point. Hence, by EGA IV2, 3.1.8, the open set \(V_t\) is schematically dense in \(G_t\). By virtue of 4.3, V is therefore schematically dense in \(G_H\). Moreover, since K is a sub-H-group of \(G_H\), it induces on each fiber \(G_h\) a subgroup \(K_h\), and since the latter contains an open neighborhood of the neutral element and \(G_h\) is connected, it follows that \(K_h = G_h\), whence \(K = G_H\) set-theoretically. Thus K is a closed subprescheme of \(G_H\) which contains the schematically dense subprescheme V, hence \(K = G_H\), which proves a).

Finally, b) is a direct consequence of 3.9, taking into account that to verify that a subprescheme K of \(P = G \times_S H\) is identical to P, it suffices to verify that for every base change \(S' \to S\), where S′ is an artinian quotient of a local ring of S, we have \(K' = P'\).

pages.title: 6. Monomorphisms of groups of multiplicative type, and canonical factorization of a homomorphism of such a group

blocks.html: 6. Monomorphisms of groups of multiplicative type, and canonical factorization of a homomorphism of such a group

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.1

Let S be a quasi-compact prescheme, G a commutative S-group prescheme, of finite presentation and quasi-finite over S. Then there exists an integer \(n > 0\) such that \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_G = 0\), i.e. \(G = {}_n G\).

If S is the spectrum of a field k, then G is finite over k, and by VIIA 8.5, it suffices to take \(n = \deg(G/k)\). Suppose now \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\) local artinian; let k be the residue field of A, \(G_0 = G \otimes_A k\), and \(n_0 = \deg(G_0/k)\). Let us distinguish two cases.

a) k is of characteristic zero. Then \(G_0\) is separable over k, hence G is unramified over S. Then the unit section of G is an open immersion, hence \({}_n G = \mathrm{Ker}(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_G)\) is an open subscheme of G, so for it to equal G, it suffices that it do so set-theoretically; we may therefore then take \(n = n_0\).

b) k is of characteristic \(p > 0\). Denote by \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximal ideal of A and \(S_r = \mathrm{Spec}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{r+1})\) for every r. Let m be an integer such that \(\mathfrak{m}^{m+1} = 0\); I claim that one can take \(n = p^m n_0\). Indeed, proceeding by induction on m, and setting \(n' = p^{m-1} n_0\), we may suppose that \(n' \cdot \mathrm{id}_G\) induces in \(G_{m-1} = G \times_S S_{m-1}\) the zero endomorphism.39 Denote by E the set of endomorphisms u of G having this property and by K the kernel of the morphism of group functors \(G \to \prod_{S_{m-1}/S} G\) (cf. III 0.1.2). Then E is identified with \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,K)\); in particular the abelian group law on E is induced by that of K. Now, by III 0.9, K is the S-group functor which to every \(g : T \to S\) associates the k-vector space \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{T_0}}\big(g_0^*(\Omega^1_{G_0/S_0}), \mathfrak{m}^m \mathscr{O}_T\big)\,; \] we therefore have \(p u = 0\) for every \(u \in E\). Hence we have \(p n' \cdot \mathrm{id}_G = 0\), i.e. \(p^m n_0 \cdot \mathrm{id}_G = 0\).
Suppose now S noetherian (one reduces to this case in 6.1 by the usual reduction to the noetherian case40). It then suffices to combine what precedes with the following

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2

Let X be a prescheme quasi-finite over S noetherian, and consider an increasing filtered family of subpreschemes \((X^i)_{i \in I}\) having the following property:

for every \(s \in S\) and \(n \geqslant 0\), setting \(S_{s,n} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\), there exists an \(i \in I\) such that \(X^i \times_S S_{s,n} = X \times_S S_{s,n}\).

Under these conditions, there exists an \(i \in I\) such that \(X^i = X\).

Since S is noetherian, there exists a maximal open set U such that \(X|_U = X^i|_U\) for i large; we are going to show that \(U = S\). In other words, we are going to show that if \(U \neq S\), one can find a U′ strictly larger than U, and an i such that \(X|_{U'} = X^i|_{U'}\). Localizing at a maximal point s of \(S - U\), we are reduced to the case where S is local with closed point s, and \(U = S - \{s\}\). (Indeed, if we set \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{S,s})\) and if there exists an i such that \(X \times_S S' = X^i \times_S S'\), then41 there exists an open neighborhood V of s such that \(X|_V = X^i|_V\), so taking i large enough that \(X^i|_U = X|_U\), we shall have \(X|_W = X^i|_W\), where \(W = U \cup V\)).

Then, for i large, since \(X|_U = X^i|_U\), one sees that \(X^i\) is a closed subprescheme of X defined by an ideal \(\mathscr{I}^{(i)}\) with support contained in \(X_s = X_0 = X \times_S S_0\) (where \(S_n = \mathrm{Spec}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\), \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\)). Since \(X_0\) is quasi-finite over \(S_0\), \(X_0\) is a finite closed subset of the noetherian X, hence \(\mathscr{I}^{(i)}\) is a module of finite length. It follows that there exists an integer \(n \geqslant 0\) such that \(\mathscr{I}^{(i)} \cap \mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\mathscr{O}_X = 0\). On the other hand, by virtue of the hypothesis in 6.2, we may suppose (upon increasing i) that the image of \(\mathscr{I}^{(i)}\) in \(\mathscr{O}_{X_n} = \mathscr{O}_X/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\mathscr{O}_X\) is zero. This implies \(\mathscr{I}^{(i)} = 0\), hence \(X^i = X\). Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3

Let S be a prescheme, K a group prescheme over S, locally of finite presentation, \(s \in S\) such that \(K_s\) is quasi-finite (resp. unramified) over \(\kappa(s)\) at the unit element. Then there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(K|_U\) is locally quasi-finite (resp. unramified) over U.*

Indeed, let V be the set of points x of K such that, denoting by t the image of x in S, the fiber \(K_t\) is quasi-finite (resp. unramified) over \(\kappa(t)\) at x, i.e. such that x is isolated in \(K_t\) (resp. and its local ring in \(K_t\) a separable extension of \(\kappa(t)\)). One knows that V is open since K is locally of finite presentation over S,42 so if \(\varepsilon\) denotes the unit section of K, \(\varepsilon^{-1}(V)\) is open. By hypothesis it contains s, hence it is an open neighborhood U of s. The latter does the job; in other words, \(t \in U\) implies that \(K_t\) is locally quasi-finite (resp. unramified) over \(\kappa(t)\): indeed, since \(K_t\) is a group locally of finite type over \(\kappa(t)\), this results from the fact that it is quasi-finite (resp. unramified) over \(\kappa(t)\) at the point \(\varepsilon(t)\), cf. VIB 1.3.
Combining 6.1 and 6.3, we find the following

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.4

Let S be a prescheme, H an S-group of multiplicative type and of finite type, K a closed sub-group prescheme, of finite presentation over S. Let \(s \in S\) be such that \(K_s\) is finite.

Then there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(K|_U\) is contained in \({}_n H|_U\) for a certain n, and a fortiori (\({}_n H\) being finite over S) such that \(K|_U\) is finite over U.
Using Nakayama's lemma, we deduce from this:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.5

With the preceding notation, suppose that \(K_s\) is the unit group. Then there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(K|_U\) is the unit group.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6

Let \(u : H \to G\) be a homomorphism of S-group preschemes, with H of multiplicative type and of finite type, and G separated over S. We suppose moreover S locally noetherian, or G locally of finite presentation43 over S.

Let \(s \in S\) be such that \(u_s : H_s \to G_s\) is a monomorphism; then there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that u induces a monomorphism \(H|_U \to G|_U\).

Indeed, let \(K = \mathrm{Ker}(u)\); the hypothesis on \(u_s\) means that \(K_s\) is the unit group, and the conclusion that \(K|_U\) is the unit group, for suitable U. Now, G being separated over S, K is a closed subgroup of H, and in the case where one does not suppose S locally noetherian but instead G locally of finite presentation over S, K is locally of finite presentation over S43, hence of finite presentation over S since it is separated over S (H being so) and quasi-compact over S (being closed in H, which is quasi-compact over S). We may therefore apply 6.5, whence 6.6.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.6.1

44 Under the hypotheses of 6.6, note that when moreover G is affine over S (resp. of finite presentation over S), \(H|_U \to G|_U\) is even a closed immersion, as pointed out in 2.5 (resp. 2.6).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.7

Let \(u : H \to G\) be a homomorphism of S-group preschemes, with H of multiplicative type and of finite type; we suppose S locally noetherian or G locally of finite presentation over S.

If all the homomorphisms induced on the fibers \(u_s : H_s \to G_s\) are monomorphisms, then u is a monomorphism.

The reasoning is the same as in 6.6, the hypothesis that G be separated over S being unnecessary here to ensure that K is closed in H, since the hypothesis that the \(u_s\) are monomorphisms implies that K is set-theoretically reduced to the unit section of G.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.8

Let \(u : H \to G\) be a homomorphism of S-group preschemes, with H of multiplicative type and of finite type, and G separated over S. We suppose moreover S locally noetherian or G of finite presentation over S.

Then \(K = \mathrm{Ker}(u)\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type and of finite type of H, and u factors as \[ H \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } H/K \xrightarrow{\ u''\ } G, \] where H/K is of multiplicative type and of finite type, \(u'\) is the canonical homomorphism and is faithfully flat (and affine), and \(u''\) is a monomorphism.

(N.B. As remarked in 6.6.1, \(u''\) is a closed immersion if G is affine over S or of finite presentation over S). It suffices to prove that K is of multiplicative type, the rest of the proposition then resulting from 2.7 and IV 5.2.6.

Suppose first G of finite presentation over S. This hypothesis being stable under base change, we are reduced, to prove that K is of multiplicative type, to the case where H is diagonalizable, i.e. \(H = \mathbf{D}_S(M)\), where M is a finitely generated commutative group. Let \(s \in S\); then \(K_s\) is a closed subgroup of \(H_s = \mathbf{D}_{\kappa(s)}(M)\), hence by virtue of 8.145 is of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{\kappa(s)}(N)\), where N is a quotient group of M. Set \(K' = \mathbf{D}_S(N)\); then K′ is a subgroup of multiplicative type of H; let \(v' : K' \to G\) be induced by u.46 Then \(v'_s\) is the unit homomorphism by construction, hence by virtue of 5.2 there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(v'|_U : K'|_U \to G|_U\) is the unit homomorphism. Hence, replacing S by U if necessary, we may suppose that \(v'\) is the unit homomorphism, hence that u factors as \[ H \xrightarrow{\ u'\ } H/K' \xrightarrow{\ u''\ } G. \] Now, since \(u''_s\) is deduced from \(u_s\) by factoring through \(H_s \to H_s/\mathrm{Ker}(u_s)\), \(u''_s\) is a monomorphism (IV 5.2.6), hence by virtue of 6.6 there exists an open neighborhood U of s such that \(u''|_U : (H/K')|_U \to G|_U\) is a monomorphism. Hence, shrinking S if necessary, one sees that \(u''\) is a monomorphism, hence \(\mathrm{Ker}(u) = \mathrm{Ker}(u') = K'\), which proves that \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u\) is of multiplicative type.

The same proof is valid if, instead of supposing G of finite presentation over S, one supposes S locally noetherian, at least in the case where H is diagonalizable. In the case where one does not make this hypothesis on H, one must show that one can find a covering base change \(S' \to S\), with S′ locally noetherian, which trivializes H. This is indeed what we shall see in the next exposé (X 4.6).

N.D.E.: We have corrected \(X - \{s\}\) above to \(S - \{s\}\). On the other hand, recall that a quasi-finite morphism is also assumed to be of finite type, cf. EGA II, 6.2.3 (and EGA III2, ErrIII 20 for the definition of "locally quasi-finite"). Hence here (S being noetherian), X is of finite presentation over S, each immersion \(X^i \hookrightarrow X\) is of finite type (by EGA I, 6.3.5), hence \(X^i\) is of finite presentation over S, and one can apply EGA IV3, 8.8.2.
N.D.E.: cf. VI<sub>B</sub> 2.5 (ii) for a more general statement.
N.D.E.: cf. EGA IV3, 13.1.4, and EGA IV4, 17.4.1. Moreover, we have denoted by t (instead of s) the image of x.
