pages.title: 7. Algebraicity of formal homomorphisms into an affine group

blocks.html: 7. Algebraicity of formal homomorphisms into an affine group

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.1

Let A be a noetherian ring, equipped with an ideal I such that A is separated and complete for the I-adic topology, \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A)\), \(S_n = \mathrm{Spec}(A/I^{n+1})\), H, G S-group preschemes, with H of isotrivial multiplicative type, and G affine.

Then the canonical map \[ \theta : \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G) \longrightarrow \varprojlim_n \mathrm{Hom}_{S_n\text{-gr.}}(H_n,G_n) \qquad (\mathrm{x}) \] is bijective (where \(H_n, G_n\) are the \(S_n\)-groups deduced from H, G by the base change \(S_n \to S\)).

Suppose first H diagonalizable, hence of the form \[ H = \mathrm{Spec}\big(A^{(M)}\big), \] where \(B = A^{(M)}\) is the algebra of the commutative group M with coefficients in A. We also have \(G = \mathrm{Spec}(C)\), where C is an A-algebra equipped with a diagonal map (satisfying the well-known axioms). Then the homomorphisms of S-groups \(H \to G\) correspond bijectively to the homomorphisms of A-algebras \(\varphi : C \to B\) compatible with the diagonal maps, i.e. such that, for every \(f \in C\), \[ \Delta_H(\varphi(f)) = (\varphi \otimes \varphi)(\Delta_G(f)) \] where \(\Delta_H\) and \(\Delta_G\) are the diagonal maps. We have an analogous description for the homomorphisms of \(S_n\)-groups \(H_n \to G_n\), defined by certain homomorphisms of \(A_n\)-algebras \(\varphi_n : C_n \to B_n\) (where we set \(A_n = A/I^{n+1}\), \(B_n = B \otimes_A A_n\), \(C_n = C \otimes_A A_n\)). Set \[ \widehat{B} = \varprojlim_n B_n \qquad \text{and} \qquad \widehat{C} = \varprojlim_n C_n\,, \] then \(\widehat{B} = \varprojlim_n A_n^{(M)}\) is identified with a submodule of the product \(A^M\) (namely, the one consisting of the families \((a_m)_{m \in M}\) of elements of A which tend to 0 (for the I-adic topology) along the filter of complements of finite subsets of M). This already implies that the canonical homomorphism \(B \to \widehat{B}\) is injective,47 since the projective system \(\theta(\varphi) = (\varphi_n)_{n \geqslant 0}\) defines a homomorphism \(\widehat{\varphi} : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{B}\) such that the diagram below is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^{\varphi} \ar[d] & B \ar[d] \\ \widehat{C} \ar[r]^{\widehat{\varphi}} & \widehat{B} } \]

Let us prove that \(\theta\) is surjective: take a projective system \((\varphi_n)_{n \geqslant 0}\) and let us prove that it comes by reduction from a \(\varphi\) of the left-hand side. A priori, \((\varphi_n)\) defines a homomorphism on the completed algebras \[ \widehat{\varphi} : \widehat{C} \longrightarrow \widehat{B}, \] and everything comes down to seeing that its composite \(\Phi : C \to \widehat{C} \xrightarrow{\widehat{\varphi}} \widehat{B}\) with the canonical homomorphism \(C \to \widehat{C}\) maps C into B. Indeed, if this is so, we find a homomorphism of A-algebras \(\varphi : C \to B\), reducing to the \(\varphi_n\), whence we conclude at once that it is compatible with the diagonal maps (since the \(\varphi_n\) are, and since \(B \otimes_A B \to \widehat{B \otimes_A B}\) is injective, as one sees as above by replacing M by \(M \times M\)).

Note that the diagonal homomorphism \(\Delta_H\) of H defines, on passing to completions, a homomorphism \[ \widehat{\Delta}_H : \widehat{B} \longrightarrow \widehat{B \otimes_A B}, \] and we have a commutative diagram (deduced by passage to the projective limit from the analogous diagrams defined by the \(\varphi_n\)): \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^{\Phi} \ar[d]_{\Delta_G} & \widehat{B} \ar[d]^{\widehat{\Delta}_H} \\ C \otimes_A C \ar[r]^{\Psi} & \widehat{B \otimes_A B} } \] where \(\Psi\) is the composite \[ C \otimes_A C \xrightarrow{\ \Phi \otimes \Phi\ } \widehat{B} \otimes_A \widehat{B} \longrightarrow \widehat{B \otimes_A B} \] (the last arrow being the obvious canonical homomorphism). It follows that for every \(f \in C\), \(\Phi(f)\) is an element of \(\widehat{B}\) whose image under \(\widehat{\Delta}_H\) is a "decomposable" element of \(\widehat{B \otimes_A B}\), i.e. lies in the image of \(\widehat{B} \otimes_A \widehat{B}\).

Denote by \((e_m)_{m \in M}\) the canonical basis of \(A^{(M)}\) and by \((e_{m,m'})\) that of \(A^{(M \times M)} = A^{(M)} \otimes_A A^{(M)}\). Since \(\Delta_H(e_m) = e_m \otimes e_m = e_{m,m}\) for every m, it now suffices to apply the following

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.2

Let A be a noetherian ring, M a set, \((a_{m,m'})\) a family of elements of A indexed by \(M \times M\), such that

(i) \(a_{m,m'} = 0\) if \(m \neq m'\) (i.e. the support of the family is contained in the diagonal of \(M \times M\)),

(ii) We have \[ a_{m,m'} = \sum_i b^i_m c^i_{m'} \] where the \(b^i, c^i\) are finitely many elements of \(A^M\) (i.e. \((a_{m,m'})\) belongs to the image of the canonical homomorphism \(A^M \otimes_A A^M \to A^{M \times M}\)).

Under these conditions, the support of the family \((a_{m,m'})\) is finite.

By virtue of (i), the family \((a_{m,m'})\) is determined by knowing the \(a_m = a_{m,m}\). Set, for every \(x = (x_m)_{m \in M} \in A^{(M)}\): \[ (u \cdot x)_m = \sum_{m'} a_{m,m'} x_{m'}, \] i.e. interpret \((a_{m,m'})\) as the matrix of a homomorphism \(u : A^{(M)} \to A^M\). Then by virtue of (i) we simply have \[ (u \cdot x)_m = a_m x_m. \] On the other hand, by virtue of (ii) we have \[ u \cdot x \in \sum_i A \cdot b^i, \] hence \(u(A^{(M)})\) remains within a finitely generated A-module. Consequently, denoting by \((e^m)_{m \in M}\) the canonical basis of \(A^{(M)} \subset A^M\), the \(a_m e^m\) remain within a finitely generated A-module. Since A is noetherian, the module they generate is itself finitely generated, which implies (since the \(e^m\) are linearly independent) that all the \(a_m\) except finitely many are zero. This proves 7.2 and consequently 7.1 in the case where H is diagonalizable.

Let us now prove the general case of 7.1, where we suppose only H isotrivial, i.e. that there exists a finite étale surjective morphism \(S' \to S\) such that \(H' = H \times_S S'\) is diagonalizable. We shall use only the fact that \(S' \to S\) is finite and covering (for the faithfully flat and quasi-compact topology, or simply for the canonical topology of (Sch)) — thus the hypothesis "étale" could be replaced by "flat".

Let \(S'' = S' \times_S S'\); let us likewise introduce \(S'_n\) and \(S''_n = S'' \times_S S_n = S'_n \times_{S_n} S'_n\), and \(H', G', H'', G'', H'_n, G'_n, H''_n, G''_n\) deduced from H and G by the base changes one can guess. Note that H′ and H″ are now diagonalizable. Note also that S′, hence S″, is affine, and that if \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(A')\), \(S'' = \mathrm{Spec}(A'')\), then A′ and A″ are separated and complete for the topology defined by \(IA'\) resp. by \(IA''\) (since A′ and A″ are finite over A). Since \(S' \to S\) and \(S'_n \to S_n\) are covering, we obtain a commutative diagram of maps of sets whose two rows are exact: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(H,G) \ar[d]_{u} \ar[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_{S'\text{-gr.}}(H',G') \ar[d]_{u'} \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & \mathrm{Hom}_{S''\text{-gr.}}(H'',G'') \ar[d]^{u''} \\ \varprojlim_n \mathrm{Hom}_{S_n\text{-gr.}}(H_n,G_n) \ar[r] & \varprojlim_n \mathrm{Hom}_{S'_n\text{-gr.}}(H'_n,G'_n) \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & \varprojlim_n \mathrm{Hom}_{S''_n\text{-gr.}}(H''_n,G''_n) } \] (N.B. the second row is exact as a projective limit of exact diagrams, relative to the various \(S'_n \to S_n\)). By what we have already proved (the diagonalizable case), \(u'\) and \(u''\) are bijective. Hence so is u, which completes the proof of 7.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.3

Under the conditions of 7.1, suppose moreover G smooth over S, and let \(u_0 : H_0 \to G_0\) be a homomorphism of \(S_0\)-groups. Then there exists a homomorphism of S-groups \(u : H \to G\) lifting \(u_0\). Two such liftings \(u, u'\) are conjugate by an element g of \(G(S)\) reducing to the unit element of \(G_0(S_0)\).

This results from combining 3.6 and 7.1. To construct a u, one constructs \(u_n\) step by step, which is possible by 3.6, and by virtue of 7.1 the system \((u_n)\) comes from a u. Given two liftings u and \(u'\), to construct g such that \(u' = \mathrm{int}(g)u\), \(g_0 = 1\), one constructs step by step elements \(g_n\) such that \(g_0 = 1\), \(g_n\) is deduced from \(g_{n+1}\) by reduction, and \(u'_n = \mathrm{int}(g_n)u_n\); this is possible thanks to 3.6. Since A is separated and complete, the \(g_n\) come from a \(g \in G(S)\), and to prove that \(u' = \mathrm{int}(g)u\), it suffices to use the injectivity in the assertion 7.1.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 7.4

One may compare 7.1 with EGA III 5.4.1, which implies that the statement 7.1 is valid if, instead of supposing H of multiplicative type and G affine, one supposes H proper over S, and G separated and locally of finite type over S. Having a statement like 7.1 without a properness hypothesis is rather exceptional, and must here be interpreted as one of the aspects of the great "rigidity" of the structure of a group of multiplicative type. The analogous statement with \(G = H = \mathbb{G}_a\) (the additive group) is false in general, as one sees by taking A of characteristic \(p > 0\), and defining the \(u_n\) by reduction mod \(I^{n+1}\) starting from an additive formal series \[ u(\mathrm{T}) = \sum_{n \geqslant 0} a_n \mathrm{T}^{p^n}, \] where the \(a_n\) are elements of A which tend to 0 for the I-adic topology, but not for the discrete topology. The statement 7.1 also becomes false if one drops the hypothesis that G is affine, even for \(H = \mathbb{G}_m\) (the multiplicative group); one sees an example of this (with A a complete discrete valuation ring) by starting from an elliptic curve over the field of fractions K of A, which reduces (in the reduction theory of Néron-Kodaira, say) to the group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) over the residue field k:48 one will thus have a smooth commutative group scheme G over S, whose special fiber is \(\mathbb{G}_{m,k}\) (which makes it possible, thanks to 3.6, to define a projective system of isomorphisms \(u_n : H_n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } G_n\), where \(H = \mathbb{G}_{m,A}\)), but whose generic fiber is an abelian variety, so that there exists no homomorphism of S-groups \(H \to G\) other than 0.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: We have expanded what follows.
N.D.E.: One could make such an example explicit…

pages.title: 8. Subgroups, quotient groups and extensions of groups of multiplicative type over a field*

blocks.html: 8. Subgroups, quotient groups and extensions of groups of multiplicative type over a field*

blocks.title: Scholium
blocks.html:
Scholium 8.0

49 Let k be a field, H an affine k-group scheme. We denote by \(k[H]\) its affine algebra and by \(\mathrm{X}(H) = \mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-gr.}}(H, \mathbb{G}_{m,k})\) the group of characters of H. By the lemma on independence of characters, \(\mathrm{X}(H)\) is a linearly independent subset of \(k[H]\). It follows that H is diagonalizable if and only if \(\mathrm{X}(H)\) generates \(k[H]\) as a k-vector space.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.1

Let G be a diagonalizable group scheme (resp. a group scheme of multiplicative type) over a field k50. Then for every sub-group scheme H of G, H and G/H are diagonalizable (resp. of multiplicative type).

Definition 1.1 reduces us at once to the non-resp. case. An easy passage to the limit, using VIB 11.13 and VIII 3.1, reduces us to the case where G is of finite type over k51. By virtue of VIB 11.16,52 one can find a finite family of nonzero elements \[ f_i = \sum_m a_{im}\, m, \qquad a_{im} \in k \qquad (8.1.1) \] of the affine ring \(k^{(M)}\) of \(G = \mathbf{D}_k(M)\), such that the points of H (with values in an arbitrary k-algebra \(k'\)) are the points \(g \in G(k')\) such that \[ \tau_g f_i = \lambda_i(g)\, f_i, \qquad \text{with } \lambda_i(g) \in k' \qquad (8.1.2) \] where \(\tau_g\) denotes translation by g. Now we have \[ \tau_g f_i = \sum_m a_{im}\, \chi_m(g)\, m \qquad (8.1.3) \] \(\chi_m : G \to \mathbb{G}_{m,k}\) being the character associated with m, so that (8.1.2) is equivalent to the relation \[ \chi_m(g) = \chi_{m'}(g) \qquad \text{if } m, m' \in \mathrm{Z}_i, \qquad (8.1.4) \] \(\mathrm{Z}_i\) denoting the set of \(m \in M\) such that \(a_{im} \neq 0\). This relation can also be written \(\chi_{m'-m}(g) = 1\) if \(m, m' \in \mathrm{Z}_i\).

Denoting by N the subgroup of M generated by all the \(m' - m\) (i variable, and \(m, m' \in \mathrm{Z}_i\)), one deduces from the definition of \(\mathbf{D}_k(M)\) (\(\simeq\) G) that H is identified with \(\mathbf{D}_k(M/N)\). It then results from VIII 3.1 that G/H is identified with \(\mathbf{D}_k(N)\). Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.2

53 Let k be a field, H, K k-group schemes of multiplicative type and of finite type, and G a k-group scheme such that we have an exact sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow H \longrightarrow G \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow 1 \] (which implies that G is of finite type over k).

(i) If we suppose G commutative or K connected, then G is of multiplicative type.

(ii) If K and H are diagonalizable, K being a torus, then G is diagonalizable.

For the proof of (i), the reader is referred to XVII 7.1.1, of which 8.2 (i) is a special case; the case of a field is treated in part (i) of the proof of XVII 7.1.1 (which does not use the results of the following exposés).

(ii) Suppose now K and H diagonalizable: \[ K \simeq \mathbf{D}_k(M) \qquad \text{and} \qquad H \simeq \mathbf{D}_k(N), \] and suppose G of multiplicative type (which is the case by (i) if K is a torus). Then, by X 1.4, G is isotrivial over k, i.e. there exists a finite separable extension \(k'/k\) such that \(G' = G \times_k k'\) is diagonalizable, so \(G' = \mathbf{D}_{k'}(E)\), for a commutative group E, and we have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathbf{D}_{k'}(N) \longrightarrow \mathbf{D}_{k'}(E) \longrightarrow \mathbf{D}_{k'}(M) \longrightarrow 0. \qquad (1) \] Hence, by VIII, 3.1 and 3.2, M is a subgroup of E and we have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow E \longrightarrow N \longrightarrow 0. \qquad (2) \] For a given extension \(E_0\) of N by M, consider the diagonalizable k-group \(G_0 = \mathbf{D}_k(E_0)\); then the k-group functor A of automorphisms of the extension \[ 1 \longrightarrow H \longrightarrow G_0 \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow 1 \qquad (3) \] i.e. the sub-group functor of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{k\text{-gr.}}(G_0)\) whose points over a k-prescheme T are the \(\phi \in \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{T\text{-gr.}}(G_{0,T})\) inducing the identity on \(H_T\) and on \(K_T\), is identified with \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-gr.}}(K,H)\), which is, by VIII 1.5, the constant k-group with value \(\mathrm{L} = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(N,M)\).

One thus sees that the classification of the extensions G of K by H which, over a separable closure \(k_s\) of k, become isomorphic to the extension (3), is the same as that of k-torsors for the étale topology under the constant group \(\mathrm{L}_k\). Writing \(\Gamma = \mathrm{Gal}(k_s/k)\), these are classified by the Galois cohomology group (L being a trivial \(\Gamma\)-module): \[ \mathrm{H}^1_{\text{ét}}(k, \mathrm{L}_k) = \mathrm{H}^1(\Gamma, \mathrm{L}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.\,top.}}(\Gamma, \mathrm{L}). \] If moreover K is a torus, M is torsion-free, hence so is L, whence \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.\,top.}}(\Gamma, \mathrm{L}) = 0\); it follows that every extension of K by H is already diagonalizable.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 8.3

As already pointed out in the proof of 8.2, the first assertion is stated and proved over an arbitrary base in XVII 7.1.1. On the other hand, the second assertion generalizes, with essentially the same proof, to the case of an integral normal base scheme (or more generally, a geometrically unibranch one), using X 5.13 below.

Finally, one may also regard 8.1 as a corollary of the (distinctly less trivial) result 6.8, likewise valid over an arbitrary base scheme.54
